result:
The silence began at 3 a.m., but not the quiet kind. This was an absolute, vacuum-sealed silence, so profound it had a texture. Leo first noticed it because his refrigerator, the one that hummed with the grumpy, asthmatic wheeze of a thirty-year-old compressor, had stopped. Not with a click or a sputter, but as if it had never hummed at all. He held his breath. No distant growl of a garbage truck, no sigh of the central heating, no faint, tinny bleed of music from a neighbor’s sleepless night. The absence was so complete it rang in his ears.

He padded to the window, the floorboards cold and silent under his feet. The city outside was a diorama. Streetlights cast their orange pools, but no cars moved through them. The traffic lights cycled uselessly: red, yellow, green, red. No one obeyed. No one was there to obey. A newspaper, caught mid-flight hours before, was frozen against a chain-link fence. Leo’s own breath fogged the glass, but when he wiped it, the view didn’t change. He was the last living thing in a world paused on a single frame.

The first thought was panic, sharp and metallic. He fumbled for his phone. No signal. The landline emitted a dead, hollow hiss. The panic crested, broke, and then receded, leaving behind a strange, flat calm. If this was the end, it was remarkably peaceful.

He decided to make tea. The ritual of it—boiling water in the quiet kettle (gas still worked, a small, roaring miracle), steeping the leaves—was an anchor. He sat at his kitchen table, the only soul in the universe, and drank his Earl Grey. The steam warmed his face. He wondered, idly, if he should go out. See the scope of it. But a deeper instinct held him at the table. To go out would be to admit the scale. Here, in his kitchen, he could pretend it was a local phenomenon. A strange blackout. A temporary blip.

Hours passed, or what felt like hours. The light didn’t change; the static, predawn gloom held. He found himself cataloging the objects in his kitchen. The chip in the blue glaze of his favorite mug, a souvenir from a seaside town he couldn’t now name. The faint stain of red wine on the pine table, a memory of a dinner party and an argument about politics that now seemed cosmically trivial. The postcard from his sister, stuck to the fridge with a banana magnet: *Wish you were here!* He was, suddenly, everywhere and nowhere.

A memory surfaced, unbidden. He was seven, playing alone in his grandparents’ vast, sun-dappled backyard. He had been an astronaut, stranded on a distant planet. The planet was the rock garden; the spaceship, the rusty swing set. For hours, he enacted a drama of survival and exploration, talking to himself, singing songs to stave off the loneliness of the cosmos. He remembered the precise, delicious feeling of being utterly, completely alone, yet full of a world of his own making. He had *chosen* that solitude. This was different. This was solitude chosen for him.

He stood up, decision made. He would not wait in his kitchen. He would go to the heart of it. He pulled on a coat and stepped out into the hallway of his apartment building. The silence was even thicker here, a physical pressure. He walked down the five flights, his footsteps the only percussion in the world. The lobby was empty. The security guard’s chair was vacant, a half-finished crossword abandoned on the desk. *Seven letters: “A profound absence.”* He knew the answer now.

Pushing through the heavy glass doors, he entered the street. The air was still, temperatureless. He walked down the middle of the avenue, a king of emptiness. He saw cars, perfectly parked, some with drivers suspended mid-gesture, hands frozen on wheels. But no drivers. No people. Birds hung in the sky like specks of ash. He reached out to touch a lamppost; it was just a lamppost.

His feet carried him to the park. It was the same. Bronze statues held their poses for an audience of no one. The pond was a sheet of dull glass. He sat on his usual bench, the one where he ate sandwiches on Tuesdays. And he waited. For what, he didn’t know. A sound. A sign. An end.

But nothing came. Just the immense, patient silence.

And then, a shift. Not in the world, but in him. The fear, the loneliness, the desperate need for an explanation… it all began to soften at the edges. In its place grew a vast and terrifying curiosity. If he was the last, then he was also the first. The first witness to a world without its noise. He listened to the silence, truly listened. And he began to hear things within it. Not sounds, but the *potential* for sound. The memory of wind in leaves. The ghost of a child’s laugh from a forgotten summer. The echo of all the words ever spoken on this street, layered into a faint, shimmering resonance.

He was not in an empty world. He was in a world full of echoes. Every surface held a story. The scuff on the bench spoke of a hurried shoelace. The gum flattened on the path spoke of a moment’s indecision. The city was not dead; it was dreaming. And he was the only one awake to hear its dreams.

A thought occurred to him, so simple it was breathtaking. Maybe this wasn’t about the disappearance of everyone else. Maybe it was about the appearance of everything else. The world had been talking all along, shouting its history, its minute-by-minute life, but it had been drowned out by the cacophony of the living. Now, with the orchestra gone, he could hear the hall itself. The acoustics of existence.

He stood up from the bench, a new purpose humming in his veins. He would be the cartographer of this quiet world. He would walk its length and breadth and listen. He would record the stories in the cracks of the pavement, in the tilt of a window blind, in the half-read book left open on a café table. He would learn the biography of his city through its abandoned details. He was no longer the last man. He was the listener.

He began to walk, not as a lost soul, but as an explorer. He turned his face up to the unmoving sky. The silence was no longer a void. It was a vessel. And for the first time in his life, or perhaps for the first time since he was a boy playing astronaut, Leo felt completely, unutterably full. The world had emptied itself so that he could finally hear its heart beating, a slow, deep, patient pulse beneath the frozen skin of things. He had a thousand words to describe it, and a thousand more after that. He had all the words in the world. And so he began.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
